@MarioNawfal We should act while it is still possible

text:
I agree that Wikipedia cannot be used as a definitive source for Community Notes, as the editorial control there is extremely left-biased (and nihilistic imo).

Actual source material, not derivative, matters much more.
embedded_text: Hey @elonmusk, as a critical co-founder of Wikipedia, I find disturbing the influence that Wikipedia has over the Community Notes on X. Why not ask the people, “Should Wikipedia be banned as a usable source for Community Notes purposes on X? Vox populi, vox Dei.”

text:
This is just an early beta, but it’s already the most fun you can making images & video on Earth 💫 

And improvements are rolling out every day!
embedded_text:
Grok's Imagine UI is seriously one of the best UIs I've ever used.

I'm super excited for videos to get to Veo 3 level (or better), retain the speed of generation, and the ability to make longer videos.

This is going to transform entertainment media consumption.

wonder if Marcus Aurelius could have done more to carry Rome to greater heights or whether that was as good a job as could have been done\",\"sender\":\"USER\",\"grokMode\":\"NORMAL\",\"mediaUrls\":[]},{\"message\":\"Marcus Aurelius, reigning from 161 to 180 CE, is often hailed as one of Rome\\u2019s \\u201cFive Good Emperors,\\u201d and his rule is seen as a high point of the Pax Romana, a period of relative stability and prosperity. Evaluating whether he could have done more to elevate Rome or if he performed as well as possible requires weighing his achievements, challenges, and the constraints of his era. Let\\u2019s break it down.\\nAchievements\\nMarcus Aurelius maintained Rome\\u2019s stability during a turbulent period. His Stoic philosophy, articulated in his Meditations, didn\\u2019t just guide his personal conduct but informed his governance, emphasizing duty, justice, and rationality. He managed the empire through:\\nMilitary Successes: Despite not being a natural warrior, he led campaigns against Germanic tribes during the Marcomannic Wars (166\\u2013180 CE), securing Rome\\u2019s Danubian frontier, though not without heavy costs.\\nAdministrative Competence: He upheld the rule of law, promoted meritocratic appointments, and worked to curb corruption, maintaining the empire\\u2019s bureaucratic efficiency.\\nSocial Measures: Marcus supported measures for the poor, such as funding for orphanages and education, reflecting his commitment to the common good.\\nChallenges\\nMarcus faced extraordinary pressures that limited his ability to \\u201cdo more\\u201d:\\nAntonine Plague: Likely smallpox, this pandemic (165\\u2013180 CE) decimated the population, with estimates of 5\\u201310% mortality (possibly 10\\u201315 million deaths across the empire). It strained the economy, military recruitment, and social cohesion.\\nMarcomannic Wars: The Germanic and Sarmatian invasions required constant attention, draining resources. Marcus spent much of his reign on the frontier, not in Rome, limiting his ability to enact sweeping reforms.\\nSuccession Issue: His choice of Commodus as successor is often criticized, as Commodus\\u2019 reign (180\\u2013192 CE) was disastrous, unraveling much of Marcus\\u2019 work. However, dynastic succession was a norm, and Marcus had no other viable heirs after losing other sons.\\nCould He Have Done More?\\nTo assess whether Marcus could have pushed Rome to greater heights, consider what \\u201cgreater heights\\u201d might mean: territorial expansion, institutional reform, or cultural flourishing. Here are key areas where he might have acted differently:\\nSuccession: Marcus\\u2019 decision to name Commodus, his only surviving son, as heir followed Roman tradition but ignored Commodus\\u2019 evident flaws. He could have adopted a capable successor, as his predecessors like Trajan and Hadrian did, potentially ensuring longer-term stability. However, breaking with dynastic tradition risked political instability, as adoption wasn\\u2019t guaranteed to succeed.\\nMilitary Strategy: Marcus\\u2019 defensive approach in the Marcomannic Wars preserved the empire but didn\\u2019t yield significant territorial gains. A more aggressive push to annex Germania, as some emperors like Trajan pursued elsewhere, might have strengthened Rome\\u2019s borders long-term. Yet, the plague and overstretched legions made this risky, if not impossible.\\nEconomic and Social Reforms: The Antonine Plague crippled the economy, reducing tax revenue and labor. Marcus sold imperial assets to fund wars, but deeper economic reforms (e.g., restructuring taxation or addressing land inequality) might have bolstered resilience. However, such reforms could have alienated the elite, risking rebellion.\\nPhilosophical Legacy: While Meditations is a timeless work, Marcus didn\\u2019t institutionalize Stoicism as a broader cultural or educational framework. Promoting it more actively might have fostered a more cohesive imperial ideology, but this could have clashed with Rome\\u2019s religious pluralism.\\nConstraints and Context\\nRome\\u2019s structural issues\\u2014overreliance on slave labor, frontier vulnerabilities, and a fragile economy\\u2014were beyond one emperor\\u2019s ability to fix. The empire was vast, governing millions across diverse cultures, and Marcus inherited a system already showing strain. The plague and wars consumed his reign, leaving little room for grand projects like those of Augustus or Trajan. His Stoic emphasis on controlling what\\u2019s within one\\u2019s power shaped his pragmatic approach: he focused on stability, not utopian transformation.\\nWas It the Best Possible Job?\\nMarcus did an exceptional job given the circumstances. He maintained Rome\\u2019s cohesion through crises that could have fractured lesser reigns. His military efforts, though costly, prevented immediate collapse of the frontiers. His philosophical approach ensured he governed with integrity, avoiding the tyranny or decadence of later emperors like Nero or Domitian. However, his failure to secure a strong successor was a critical misstep, though understandable given dynastic pressures and personal loss (he and Faustina had 13 children, most of whom died young).\\nCould he have done more? Possibly, with bolder reforms or a different successor, but the plague and wars set hard limits. Rome\\u2019s peak under the \\u201cGood Emperors\\u201d was likely as high as it could sustainably go, given underlying structural weaknesses that surfaced later in the 3rd century Crisis. Marcus\\u2019 reign delayed decline rather than transcending it, which may be the most realistic outcome.\\nConclusion\\nMarcus Aurelius performed near the ceiling of what was possible. His rule preserved Rome\\u2019s strength through unprecedented challenges, but his choice of Commodus and focus on survival over transformation capped his legacy. Greater heights might have been achievable with perfect foresight\\u2014adopting a better heir or risking ambitious reforms\\u2014but such moves carried their own dangers. He balanced duty and crisis masterfully, even if Rome\\u2019s systemic limits meant \\u201cgreater heights\\u201d were likely out of reach for any emperor by 161 CE. text:
Grok Imagine prompt:

Anthropomorphic gray tabby cat riding a vintage brown bicycle with 'Wien' on the tube and a bottle attached to the frame, dressed in steampunk adventurer gear including a leather helmet with aviator goggles, over-ear headphones, a dark jacket with red strap accents, gloves, and jeans. The cat has orange eyes, a focused expression looking over its shoulder, and is holding a frothy pint glass of beer in its right paw while gripping the handlebars with the left. Strapped to its back is a bulky retro-futuristic jetpack backpack with tubes, gauges, switches, and a nozzle, with small paper notes fluttering around the cat’s ear as if caught in the wind. Background is a serene blue sky with soft clouds. Highly detailed digital illustration in a painterly style with warm lighting and vintage tones.
“Make another video of a cat on a bicycle drinking beer” 😂

text:
In a romantic pre-Raphaelite style, a tender moment unfolds between a handsome knight in gleaming silver armor and a beautiful woman in a flowing white gown adorned with flowers. 

The knight, with a sword at his side, sits astride a brown horse with a red-gold draped saddle, leaning down affectionately toward the woman, who wears a floral crown and gazes up lovingly with long hair cascading. 

She gently touches his armored hand, their faces drawing closer in a soft, intimate kiss, while the horse shifts subtly and her gown sways in a light breeze. 

The scene is set in an enchanting garden overflowing with vibrant pink roses, colorful wildflowers, lush green foliage, and an ancient stone archway under soft sunlight. Captured in a smooth close-up shot with a subtle zoom-in, evoking a dreamy, enchanting atmosphere of timeless romance.

text:
If you zoom out a bit, we are actually seeing extremely rapid take-off of AI, but there is enough movement of people/ideas that the leading AI companies – for now – appear to be in similar positions. 

As a side note unrelated to your post, and at the risk of starting a war, I think the em dash is too long esthetically and is particularly ugly when attached directly to letters. We should use the en dash instead, at least on this platform – where italics for emphasis – do not appear in primary text.

Japan will lose almost a million people this year. 

This demographic inevitably was set in motion half a century ago and has nothing to do with AI. 

AI is the only hope for turning this around.

> Grok 4
> companions mode
> imagine

embedded_text:
Pixel Art generated by @grok Imagine is surprisingly GOOD. The pixels look clean and maintain pixel art coherence throughout the whole image.

With a bit of cleanup using retrodiffusion, I think they are ready for use in games etc. https://t.co/CA2TbB6YCj
